ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for spring chicken with Mandi rice specifically; broader 'chicken dish' term used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for grilled spring chicken component; no composite dish code available for spring chicken with Mandi rice.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a whole or half grilled spring chicken with a generous portion of Mandi rice, typically served as a main meal for one person.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) and White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878), combined in estimated ratio 55% chicken : 45% rice by weight
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for spring chicken with Mandi rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a weighted blend of roasted chicken (55% by weight, FDC ID 171477) and cooked white rice (45% by weight, FDC ID 168878), adjusted for spice seasoning and broth cooking. The input record's per_100g values (energy 146 kcal, protein 11.5 g, fat 6.9 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and have been retained with USDA as the authoritative anchor. Sodium is elevated relative to plain USDA values due to broth and seasoning; the input value of 184.6 mg/100g is plausible and retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (GI ~64) and Basmati rice (GI ~58), weighted composite estimate for mixed chicken-rice dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spring chicken with Mandi rice. GI estimated at 56 using a weighted composite: the rice component (45% of dish by weight) drives the GI, anchored to basmati rice GI of approximately 58 per University of Sydney GI Database. The chicken component (55%) contributes negligible GI. Composite dish GI estimated at ~56 (medium). Glycemic load per 650 g serving calculated as: (56 × 65 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 36.4. Available carbohydrate per serving = 10.0 g/100g × 650 g = 65 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 184.6 mg/100g; a full 650 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium broth or homemade stock when ordering, limit portion size to half a plate, and avoid adding table salt or high-sodium condiments to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 56 and a glycemic load of 36.4 per full 650 g serving; the rice component is the primary glycaemic driver.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the rice portion to approximately 100–150 g cooked (one-quarter of the standard serving) to keep the glycaemic load below 10, prioritise the grilled chicken portion, and avoid sugary sauces or gravies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.85 g/100g and trans fat is minimal at 0.08 g/100g; grilled preparation avoids deep-frying.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The grilled preparation and low saturated fat profile are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, patients should monitor total sodium intake per serving (~1,200 mg) and request olive oil substitution for any cooking oil to optimise the unsaturated fat profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, cardamom) and low saturated fat; nightshade ingredients (tomato paste) may be a concern for solanine-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile; those sensitive to nightshades should request the dish without tomato paste and ensure cooking oil is olive oil rather than palm oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spring Chicken with Mandi Rice is a Turkish-influenced grilled chicken dish served in Singapore, consisting of a whole or half young chicken (spring chicken, typically 400–600 g live weight) marinated in a blend of Turkish and Middle Eastern spices including cumin, coriander, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, ginger, and garlic, then grilled until the skin is golden and the meat is juicy and tender. It is served alongside Mandi rice — fragrant long-grain or basmati rice cooked in seasoned chicken broth with whole spices, producing a subtly aromatic, slightly sticky rice with a golden hue. The dish has a savoury, smoky, and warmly spiced flavour profile with a moist chicken texture and fluffy, fragrant rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 146 kcal, 11.5 g protein, 6.9 g fat (1.85 g saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal. The full 650 g serving delivers approximately 950 kcal and 75 g protein. Sodium is moderate at 184.6 mg/100g (~1,200 mg per serving), which is a consideration for hypertension management. The glycaemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), driven by the basmati rice component. The dish is halal-certified and gluten-free. It is commonly found on GrabFood and similar delivery platforms in Singapore, often served in Turkish or Middle Eastern casual dining restaurants.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for spring chicken with Mandi rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a weighted blend of roasted chicken and cooked white rice components, adjusted for seasoning. The input record values are broadly consistent with this estimate and have been retained with USDA as the authoritative anchor. GI is an analogous estimate based on the basmati rice component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite grilled chicken and Mandi rice dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish preparation is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite estimate used. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available as a single validated entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not constitute an approved primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central has been used as the approved anchor instead. The dish is described as Turkish-influenced but is sold in Singapore via GrabFood; cultural classification reflects Singapore as country of origin with Turkish culinary influence. Trans fat value of 0.08 g/100g is retained from input as a plausible trace value consistent with grilled chicken preparation using vegetable oil.
